chapter_info.subject: Social Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The Parliamentary System: Legislature and Executive

topics.content: India's independence was achieved through great sacrifices against colonial rule. For the first time, the citizens of a free country could make decisions about their own governance. The drafting of the Constitution was the first major step in this new epoch.
topics.additional_notes: The Constitution is described as a 'vehicle of life' by Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar.

topics.content: A foundational principle where all adult citizens have the right to vote regardless of social or economic background, gender, race, or religion. This ensures that the government functions with the people's consent.

The Indian Parliament has a 'bicameral' structure, meaning it consists of two houses: the Lok Sabha (House of the People / Lower House) and the Rajya Sabha (Council of States / Upper House). The President of India is also an integral part of the Parliament.

examples.description: 'Bi' means two, and 'cameral' means chamber or house.

topics.content: While inspired by Britain's parliamentary system, India's model also drew from ancient Indian traditions like the 'Mahajanapadas' (republics) and village panchayats where collective decisions were made.
topics.additional_notes: The new Parliament building (Sansad Bhavan) design reflects inclusivity and balance, incorporating ancient symbols like the Sengol (sceptre).

notes.section_title: The Two Houses: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha

topics.content: Members are directly elected by the people based on universal adult suffrage. The maximum number of members is 550. It is presided over by the Speaker, who is elected by the members to conduct sessions and maintain discipline.

examples.description: A Lok Sabha typically serves for 5 years. There have been 18 Lok Sabhas since 1952, with the 18th constituted in June 2024.

topics.content: Members are elected indirectly by the elected members of State Legislative Assemblies. It represents the states in the federal system. The Vice President of India is the ex-officio Chairperson of the Rajya Sabha.
topics.additional_notes: The number of seats for each state is based on its population.

topics.content: The Parliament elects the President and Vice President and has the power to amend the Constitution to meet the changing needs of society while upholding core values like federalism and fundamental rights.
One of the primary responsibilities is to frame laws. A law begins as a 'Bill' (a draft proposal) and becomes an 'Act' after being passed by both houses and receiving the President's assent.

examples.description: The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act ensures free education for children aged 6-14, fulfilling a long-standing goal of the Directive Principles.

topics.content: The Parliament keeps a check on the government's activities through the Question Hour and Standing Committees. It also monitors government expenditure through the annual budget, ensuring transparency in how taxpayer money is spent.
The process involves multiple stages: Introduction, Readings (discussion), Committee referral (optional but common for scrutiny), Clause-by-clause voting, and repetition in the second house.
topics.additional_notes: A bill must receive Presidential assent to become law. Once enacted, it is published in the Official Gazette.

topics.content: Bills concerning financial matters like taxation or government borrowing are called Money Bills. They can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha and require the prior recommendation of the President.
The Union Executive consists of the President, the Vice President, and the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister.
topics.additional_notes: It is responsible for implementing and enforcing laws passed by the Parliament.

topics.content: The Head of State and nominal head of the Executive. Most functions are performed on the advice of the Council of Ministers, but the President has discretionary powers during political crises (e.g., when no party has a majority).

topics.topic_name: Prime Minister and Council of Ministers
topics.content: The Prime Minister is the de facto executive authority. The PM leads the Council of Ministers, coordinates different ministries, and shapes national policies. They are collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

examples.description: In 1956, Lal Bahadur Shastri resigned as Railway Minister after a train accident, taking moral responsibility even though he wasn't personally blamed.

topics.content: States have a governance structure similar to the Union. The Governor is the Constitutional Head (appointed by the President), and the Chief Minister is the de facto head leading the State Council of Ministers.
topics.additional_notes: MLAs (Members of Legislative Assembly) make laws at the state level.

topics.content: Most states have a unicameral system (Vidhan Sabha only). Some states like Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Uttar Pradesh have a bicameral system (Vidhan Sabha and Vidhan Parishad).

notes.section_title: The Federal Structure: Distribution of Powers

topics.content: The Constitution divides lawmaking powers into three lists: Union List (Centre only), State List (States only), and Concurrent List (Both).

examples.description: Education and Environmental policy require collaboration between the Union and States.

topics.content: The Judiciary is the custodian of the Constitution. It interprets laws and ensures that neither the Legislature nor the Executive exceeds their constitutional powers. This system of 'checks and balances' prevents any one organ from becoming too powerful.
The Parliament is not always formal; debates often include poetry (e.g., Nirmala Sitharaman quoting Tirukkural) and sharp humour to critique the government in a healthy democratic way.

examples.description: The poetic exchange between Sushma Swaraj and Dr. Manmohan Singh in 2011 highlighted accountability through verse.

topics.content: Issues include disruptions of the Question Hour, low productivity (hours functioned vs scheduled), absenteeism, and the presence of representatives with criminal cases.
topics.additional_notes: Productivity has varied across Lok Sabhas, sometimes dipping significantly due to disruptions.

topics.content: Citizens help shape better policies by staying informed, asking questions, and participating in public debates. Technology now makes it easier for youth to engage with governance and constructively influence policy.

examples.description: Atal Bihari Vajpayee famously said: 'Governments will come and go... but the nation must endure, and its democracy must live on.'

glossary.definition: The right of all adult citizens to vote irrespective of their background.
A legislative system consisting of two houses or chambers.
A draft proposal of a law presented to the legislature for approval.
A bill that has been passed by the legislature and received the head of state's assent, becoming law.
A bill specifically concerned with financial matters like taxation or government spending.
A system of government where power is divided between a central authority and constituent units (states).
A gold-plated sceptre serving as a symbol of righteous and just rule, placed in the new Lok Sabha.
A Latin term meaning 'in fact' or 'in reality', referring to the actual power holder (e.g., the Prime Minister).
Referring to what is legal or official (e.g., the President as the nominal head).
The principle that all ministers of the government are together responsible for the decisions and actions of the executive.
The first hour of a parliamentary session where MPs ask questions to ministers about government policies.
The specific ministry or department assigned to a minister (e.g., Finance, Education).

summary_points: The Indian Parliamentary system is a bicameral structure consisting of the Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha, and the President.
The Constitution ensures representation and accountability through Universal Adult Franchise and the separation of powers.
The lawmaking process is rigorous, involving multiple stages to ensure laws like the RTE Act are thoroughly debated before enactment.
The Executive (PM and Council of Ministers) implements laws and is collectively responsible to the directly elected Lok Sabha.
India follows a federal system where power is distributed across the Union, State, and Concurrent Lists.
The Judiciary acts as a check on both the Legislature and the Executive to uphold the Constitutional framework.
The effectiveness of democracy depends on an active, informed citizenry and the productive functioning of its representative institutions.
